equipment\\germany.json:
[
  {
    "id": "mg-42",
    "name": "MG 42 General-Purpose Machine Gun",
    "type": "Infantry Weapon",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "category": "Machine Gun",
    "years_of_service": "1942–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Großfuß Metall- und Lackierwarenfabrik",
    "caliber": "7.92×57mm Mauser",
    "rate_of_fire": "1,200–1,500 rounds/min",
    "weight_kg": 11.57,
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/equipment/mg42.jpg",
    "summary": "The MG 42 was the most effective general-purpose machine gun of the war and is still in service today in modified form. Its exceptionally high rate of fire produced the characteristic tearing sound that earned it the Allied nicknames 'Hitler's Buzzsaw' and 'Bone Saw'. Allied infantry were trained to go to ground immediately upon hearing it.",
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "McNab, Chris. The MG 42 Machine Gun. Osprey, 2014." },
      { "ref": "Weeks, John. Infantry Weapons of World War II. Orbis, 1971." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "stg-44", "title": "Sturmgewehr 44", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/stg-44" },
      { "id": "kar-98k", "title": "Karabiner 98k", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/kar-98k" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "stg-44",
    "name": "Sturmgewehr 44",
    "type": "Infantry Weapon",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "category": "Assault Rifle",
    "years_of_service": "1944–1945",
    "manufacturer": "C. G. Haenel, others",
    "caliber": "7.92×33mm Kurz",
    "rate_of_fire": "500–600 rounds/min",
    "weight_kg": 5.22,
    "units_produced": 425977,
    "summary": "The StG 44 was the world's first mass-produced assault rifle, combining controllable automatic fire with the range of a rifle. It defined the concept nearly every military rifle today follows. Hitler initially opposed the project; it was developed partly in secret and presented as a 'machine pistol'.",
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Senich, Peter. The German Assault Rifle 1935–1945. Paladin Press, 1987." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "mg-42", "title": "MG 42", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/mg-42" },
      { "id": "kar-98k", "title": "Karabiner 98k", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/kar-98k" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "panzerfaust",
    "name": "Panzerfaust",
    "type": "Infantry Weapon",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "category": "Anti-tank Weapon",
    "years_of_service": "1943–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Multiple",
    "range_m": 30,
    "penetration_mm": 200,
    "weight_kg": 6.8,
    "units_produced": 8250000,
    "summary": "The Panzerfaust ('tank fist') was a cheap, single-shot, disposable anti-tank weapon requiring minimal training. Effective at short range against all Allied tanks, it became the primary anti-tank weapon of German infantry, particularly the Volkssturm in 1945.",
    "dossier": {
      "photos": [
        { "type": "image", "images": [ { "src": "/public/images/armaments/equipment/panzerfaust/photos/panzerfaust.webp", "caption": "Panzerfaust anti-tank weapon", "type": "photograph", "historical_status": "Source Unverified — Pending Archival Review", "notes": "This image's original source has not yet been verified. Provenance details will be added once confirmed." } ] }
      ]
    },
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Fleischer, Wolfgang. German Anti-Tank Weapons. Schiffer, 1999." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "pz-v-panther", "title": "Panther", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/pz-v-panther" },
      { "id": "stg-44", "title": "StG 44", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/stg-44" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "mp-40",
    "name": "MP 40 Submachine Gun",
    "type": "Infantry Weapon",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "category": "Submachine Gun",
    "years_of_service": "1940–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Erma Werke, Steyr-Daimler-Puch, Haenel",
    "caliber": "9×19mm Parabellum",
    "rate_of_fire": "500–550 rounds/min",
    "weight_kg": 4.03,
    "units_produced": 1100000,
    "summary": "The MP 40 — often incorrectly called the 'Schmeisser' — was the standard submachine gun of German NCOs and vehicle crews. Its all-metal construction and folding stock were innovations for the period. It became an iconic symbol of the German military in wartime film and photography.",
    "dossier": {
      "photos": [
        { "type": "image", "images": [ { "src": "/public/images/armaments/equipment/mp-40/photos/mp-40.webp", "caption": "MP 40 submachine gun", "description": "Studio photograph showing the weapon's left and right profiles, stock extended, with the wood-and-metal grip frame and curved magazine visible in both views.", "type": "photograph", "historical_status": "Source Unverified — Pending Archival Review", "notes": "This image's original source has not yet been verified. Provenance details will be added once confirmed." } ] }
      ]
    },
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Weeks, John. Infantry Weapons of World War II. Orbis, 1971." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "stg-44", "title": "StG 44", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/stg-44" },
      { "id": "kar-98k", "title": "Karabiner 98k", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/kar-98k" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "kar-98k",
    "name": "Karabiner 98k",
    "type": "Infantry Weapon",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "category": "Bolt-Action Rifle",
    "years_of_service": "1935–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Mauser, Gustloff-Werke, Berlin-Lübecker Maschinenfabrik, others",
    "caliber": "7.92×57mm Mauser",
    "action": "Bolt-action, 5-round internal magazine",
    "effective_range_m": 500,
    "weight_kg": 3.9,
    "length_mm": 1107,
    "units_produced": 14600000,
    "summary": "The Karabiner 98k was the standard service rifle of the Wehrmacht throughout the Second World War, produced in greater numbers than any other German small arm. A refined version of the Mauser 98 action — the most reliable bolt-action ever designed — it was accurate, robust, and capable of withstanding extreme conditions. Despite being slower to fire than the American M1 Garand semi-automatic rifle, German units equipped with MG 42s compensated at the squad level: the Kar 98k infantryman was ammunition-carrier and fire supporter for the machine gun team. Over 14.6 million were produced.",
    "sources": [
      { "ref": "Ball, Robert. Mauser Military Rifles of the World. Krause, 1996." },
      { "ref": "Law, Richard. Sniper Variations of the German K98k Rifle. Collector Grade Publications, 1996." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "stg-44", "title": "Sturmgewehr 44", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/stg-44" },
      { "id": "mg-42", "title": "MG 42", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/mg-42" },
      { "id": "rearmament", "title": "German Rearmament 1933–1939", "type": "Article", "url": "/articles/military/rearmament" }
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "flak-88",
    "name": "8.8 cm Flak 18/36/37",
    "type": "Heavy Artillery",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "category": "Anti-Aircraft / Anti-Tank Gun",
    "years_of_service": "1933–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Krupp, Rheinmetall",
    "caliber_mm": 88,
    "shell_weight_kg": 9.24,
    "muzzle_velocity_ms": 820,
    "effective_ceiling_m": 8000,
    "effective_range_anti_tank_m": 2000,
    "penetration_mm": 120,
    "weight_kg": 5000,
    "units_produced": 21310,
    "summary": "The '88' — as it was universally known — was perhaps the most feared and versatile weapon of the Second World War. Originally designed as a high-velocity anti-aircraft gun, its first use against tanks in Spain demonstrated devastating anti-armour capability. In the desert, Rommel used them as anti-tank guns when the British Matilda tanks proved impervious to all conventional German anti-tank weapons. No Allied tank was safe from the 88 at any normal combat range — it could penetrate the armour of every tank it faced. The Tiger I tank was designed around its naval variant, the KwK 36. The Flak 88 required a crew of six and was too heavy for frontline portability in anti-tank roles, but its effectiveness against aircraft and armour made it the defining weapon of German tactical doctrine.",
    "sources": [
      { "type": "primary", "ref": "Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv, Freiburg. RH 10/143: Artillery equipment records — 8.8cm Flak development." },
      { "ref": "Hogg, Ian. German Artillery of World War Two. Greenhill Books, 1997." },
      { "ref": "Jentz, Thomas. Panzer Truppen: The Complete Guide to the Creation and Combat Employment of Germany's Tank Force. Schiffer, 1996." }
    ],
    "related_records": [
      { "id": "pz-vi-tiger", "title": "Tiger I", "type": "Armament", "url": "/armaments/pz-vi-tiger" },
      { "id": "erwin-rommel", "title": "Erwin Rommel", "type": "Personnel", "url": "/personnel/erwin-rommel" },
      { "id": "gazala", "title": "Battle of Gazala", "type": "Campaign", "url": "/campaigns/gazala" },
      { "id": "britain", "title": "Battle of Britain", "type": "Campaign", "url": "/campaigns/britain" }
    ]
  }
]
equipment\\italy.json:
{
  "category": "Infantry Equipment — Italy",
  "equipment": [
    {
      "name": "Carcano M91/38",
      "type": "Bolt-Action Rifle",
      "calibre": "7.35×51mm",
      "length_mm": 1017,
      "weight_kg": 3.4,
      "magazine": "6-round en-bloc clip",
      "notes": "The standard Italian infantry rifle. The calibre was changed from 6.5mm to 7.35mm in 1938 for better stopping power, but wartime shortages meant many soldiers continued using older 6.5mm weapons."
    },
    {
      "name": "Beretta MAB 38",
      "type": "Submachine Gun",
      "calibre": "9×19mm Parabellum",
      "length_mm": 946,
      "weight_kg": 4.2,
      "magazine": "10, 20, 30, or 40-round box magazine",
      "notes": "Considered one of the finest submachine guns of the war. Adopted by Italian, German, and Romanian forces. Its double-trigger design (one for semi-auto, one for full-auto) was innovative."
    },
    {
      "name": "Breda M30",
      "type": "Light Machine Gun",
      "calibre": "6.5×52mm",
      "weight_kg": 10.5,
      "rate_of_fire": "475 rpm",
      "notes": "Italy's standard squad automatic weapon. Criticised for its unusual 20-round feed strip (which had to be oiled before loading) and tendency to jam. Replaced gradually by the Breda M37."
    },
    {
      "name": "Breda M37",
      "type": "Medium Machine Gun",
      "calibre": "8×59mm RB",
      "weight_kg": 19.4,
      "rate_of_fire": "450–500 rpm",
      "notes": "Italy's standard medium machine gun from 1937. More reliable than the M30. Used a tray feed system; fired cartridges were reinserted into the feed tray (a distinctive feature)."
    }
  ]
}
equipment\\japan.json:
{
  "category": "Infantry Equipment — Japan",
  "equipment": [
    {
      "name": "Arisaka Type 38",
      "type": "Bolt-Action Rifle",
      "calibre": "6.5×50mm",
      "length_mm": 1275,
      "weight_kg": 3.95,
      "magazine": "5-round internal box magazine",
      "notes": "The primary Japanese infantry rifle of World War II, derived from an 1897 design. The long length reflected the tradition of attaching a bayonet for close combat."
    },
    {
      "name": "Arisaka Type 99",
      "type": "Bolt-Action Rifle",
      "calibre": "7.7×58mm",
      "length_mm": 1118,
      "weight_kg": 3.8,
      "magazine": "5-round internal box magazine",
      "notes": "Replaced the Type 38 from 1939. More powerful calibre; some versions included a monopod and anti-aircraft sights. Late-war versions show significant quality decline."
    },
    {
      "name": "Type 96 Light Machine Gun",
      "type": "Light Machine Gun",
      "calibre": "6.5×50mm",
      "weight_kg": 9.07,
      "magazine": "30-round detachable box magazine",
      "rate_of_fire": "550 rpm",
      "notes": "Japan's squad automatic weapon through 1941. Required lubricated cartridges to feed reliably."
    },
    {
      "name": "Type 11 Grenade Discharger",
      "type": "Grenade Launcher",
      "calibre": "50mm",
      "weight_kg": 2.6,
      "range_m": 190,
      "notes": "Often incorrectly called a 'knee mortar' by Allied soldiers (firing it from the knee would break the leg). Shoulder-fired or braced against the ground, it gave Japanese infantry sections organic indirect fire capability."
    }
  ]
}
equipment/other-axis.json:
{
  "category": "Infantry Equipment — Other Axis Powers",
  "equipment": [
    {
      "name": "ZB vz. 26",
      "nation": "Czechoslovakia/Germany (captured)",
      "type": "Light Machine Gun",
      "calibre": "7.92×57mm",
      "weight_kg": 9.6,
      "magazine": "20 or 30-round box magazine",
      "rate_of_fire": "500 rpm",
      "notes": "Czech-designed light machine gun widely used by German forces after the occupation of Czechoslovakia. The design directly influenced the British Bren gun."
    },
    {
      "name": "Puşcă Mitralieră md. 1939 (ZB-30)",
      "nation": "Romania",
      "type": "Light Machine Gun",
      "calibre": "7.92×57mm",
      "weight_kg": 9.6,
      "magazine": "20-round box magazine",
      "notes": "Romanian-licence-produced ZB-30. Standard squad automatic weapon of the Romanian Army."
    },
    {
      "name": "Mannlicher M1895 (Repetier-Gewehr M.95)",
      "nation": "Hungary / Romania / Bulgaria",
      "type": "Bolt-Action Rifle",
      "calibre": "8×50mmR",
      "length_mm": 1272,
      "weight_kg": 3.77,
      "notes": "World War I-era Austro-Hungarian rifle retained by successor states. Straight-pull bolt action allowed fast reloading. Still in service with minor Axis partners in 1939–45."
    }
  ]
}
